# Facebook Post — OpenAI Closes $122B Funding at $852B Valuation, Kills Sora, Pivots to Enterprise
# Week: Week of 2026-03-30
# Brand Voice: GYanTech AI — Visionary, executive audience, lead generation
# Status: Review before posting
# Auto-generated: 2026-04-05T23:02:30.015Z

---

OpenAI raised $122 billion this week — then killed one of its most high-profile products.

Sora, the company's video generation tool, was shut down after its user base collapsed from 1 million to under 500,000 while burning roughly $1 million per day in compute costs. In the same breath, OpenAI announced a full pivot toward enterprise: coding tools, agent workflows, and an integrated AI Super App combining ChatGPT, Codex, browsing, and autonomous agent functions.

At an $852 billion valuation, this is now the most well-capitalized AI company in history making a deliberate choice to go narrow and deep rather than broad and experimental.

What does this mean for business leaders? Two things. First, the AI platforms you are building on today may look fundamentally different in 12 months. Product lines will be cut, merged, or repositioned as these companies fight for enterprise dominance. Second, the companies that navigate this well will be the ones that built modular AI architectures — systems designed to absorb platform shifts without starting over.

This is exactly the kind of strategic planning GYanTech AI works on with executive teams. If your AI roadmap does not account for vendor volatility, it is time to revisit it -> www.gyantechai.com

## Fact-check notes
- OpenAI announced completion of $122 billion in funding with a post-money valuation of $852 billion on March 31, 2026 (Source: Amiko Consulting weekly roundup, citing OpenAI announcement)
- OpenAI terminated Sora after users declined from ~1 million at peak to under 500,000, with the product consuming approximately $1 million per day in costs (Source: Reuters via Amiko Consulting)
- OpenAI is reallocating resources toward coding and enterprise tools in response to competition from Google and Anthropic (Source: Reuters via Amiko Consulting)
